coredata.dc:title: A UV cross-linked gel polymer electrolyte enabling high-rate and high voltage window for quasi-solid-state supercapacitors

coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Yuge
Yang, Chao
Yuan, Boheng
Li, Hongjie
Chen, Weimeng
Yin, Haosen
Zhao, Bin
Shen, Fei
Han, Xiaogang
coredata.dc:description: Serving as a promising alternative to liquid electrolyte in the application of portable and wearable devices, gel polymer electrolytes (GPEs) are expected to obtain more preferable properties rather than just be satisfied with the merits of high safety and deformability. Here, an easy-operated method is employed to fabricate cross-linked composite polymer membranes used for GPEs assisted by UV irradiation, in which N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and TiO2 are introduced as photocatalysts and additives to improve the performances of GPEs. Specifically, N-CQDs participate as a cross-linker to construct the inner porous structure, and TiO2 nanoparticles serve as a stabilizer to improve the electrochemical stability of GPEs under high voltage (3.5 V). The excellent thermal and mechanical stability of the membrane fabricated in this work guarantee the safety of the supercapacitors (SCs). This GPE based SC not only exhibits prominent rate performance (105% capacitance retention at the current density of 40 A g−1) and cyclic stability (85% at 1 A g−1 under 3.5 V after 20,000 cycles), but also displays remarkable energy density (42.88 Wh kg−1) with high power density (19.3 kW kg−1). Moreover, the superior rate and cycling performances of the as-prepared GPE based flexible SCs under flat and bending state confirm the feasibility of its application in flexible energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gel polymer electrolyte
UV cross-linking
Energy density
High voltage window

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of cross-linked PVDF-HFP composite gel polymer electrolyte

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We appreciate the funding supports from the Natural Science Basis Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (2019JLZ-10), and the Independent Research Project of National Key Laboratory of Electrical Insulation and Power Equipment (EIPE19111). The authors are grateful to Instrumental Analysis Center of Xi'an Jiaotong University for technical assistance. SEM was carried out at the Center for Advancing Materials Performance from the Nanoscale (CAMP NANO). We thank Dr. Chaowei Guo for his help with SEM.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of UV cross-linked gel polymer electrolyte and the assembly of supercapacitors.
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of cross-section of the membranes; (a) without UV irradiation, (b) with UV irradiation.
XPS spectra of C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s regions of the membranes before (a-c) and after (d-f) UV irradiation.

Characterization of the membranes with and without UV irradiation and PVDF-HFP membranes. (a) FTIR spectra of the three membranes; (b) DSC thermograms of two samples in the temperature range of 100–380 ℃; (c) TGA curves of the two prepared membranes; (d) tensile stress–strain test of three membranes; (e) frequency dependent dielectric constant.

Electrochemical performances of membranes with and without UV exposure and cellulose separator in coin cell under 2.5 V. (a) Nyquist plots of the supercapacitors fabricated using prepared two membranes and commercial cellulose separator. Cyclic voltammograms of (b) P-N-12.5P-T (W/O UV) and (c) P-N-12.5P-T (UV) samples under 2.5 V and different scan rates. (d) Rate performance and (e) cycling performance of three samples at the operational voltage of 2.5 V.

Electrochemical performances of membranes with and without UV exposure, the irradiated membrane without adding TiO2 and cellulose separator in coin cell under 3.5 V. (a) Nyquist plots of the supercapacitors fabricated using prepared three membranes and commercial cellulose separator. (b) The Electrochemical stability window for three samples. (c) Cycling performance and (d) rate performance of four samples at the operational voltage of 3.5 V. (e) Ragone plots.

Electrochemical performance of the flexible supercapacitors fabricated by using prepared GPEs under 3.5 V. (a) CV curves of the two devices under flat and bending state (bending radius: 12.5 mm) at different scan rate. (b) Rate performance of two samples under flat and bending state. (c) Photograph of a LED lights powered by P-N-12.5P-T (UV) based supercapacitors under flat and bending state. (d) Cycling stability of two GPE based supercapacitors at bending state with the current density of 0.5 mA cm−2 (inset: photograph of a LED lights powered by the bending quasi-solid-state supercapacitor with P-N-12.5P-T (UV) GPE after discharging 0 s, 60 s and 120 s respectively.).

head.ce:title: A UV cross-linked gel polymer electrolyte enabling high-rate and high voltage window for quasi-solid-state supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electrical Insulation and Power Equipment, School of Electrical Engineering, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an 710049, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electrical Insulation and Power Equipment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Smart Grid of Shaanxi Province, Xi'an 710049, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Smart Grid of Shaanxi Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Smart Grid of Shaanxi Province, Xi'an 710049, Shaanxi, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The composite membrane with porous structure applied in gel polymer electrolytes is fabricated by UV cross-linking PVDF-HFP with PAN assisted by N-CQDs and TiO2, enabling supercapacitors with superb electrochemical performance.

Serving as a promising alternative to liquid electrolyte in the application of portable and wearable devices, gel polymer electrolytes (GPEs) are expected to obtain more preferable properties rather than just be satisfied with the merits of high safety and deformability. Here, an easy-operated method is employed to fabricate cross-linked composite polymer membranes used for GPEs assisted by UV irradiation, in which N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and TiO2 are introduced as photocatalysts and additives to improve the performances of GPEs. Specifically, N-CQDs participate as a cross-linker to construct the inner porous structure, and TiO2 nanoparticles serve as a stabilizer to improve the electrochemical stability of GPEs under high voltage (3.5 V). The excellent thermal and mechanical stability of the membrane fabricated in this work guarantee the safety of the supercapacitors (SCs). This GPE based SC not only exhibits prominent rate performance (105% capacitance retention at the current density of 40 A g−1) and cyclic stability (85% at 1 A g−1 under 3.5 V after 20,000 cycles), but also displays remarkable energy density (42.88 Wh kg−1) with high power density (19.3 kW kg−1). Moreover, the superior rate and cycling performances of the as-prepared GPE based flexible SCs under flat and bending state confirm the feasibility of its application in flexible energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: With the penetration of portable devices in human life, the urgent need for light weighted and deformable electronic devices promotes the demand for new formats of energy storage devices. As a result, the energy storage devices for flexible electronics are required to be designed with high electrochemical durability and mechanical stability under varying degrees of deformation [1–3]. Among various energy storage devices, supercapacitors (SCs) are deemed as one of the strongest candidates which bear the nature of high power density, prolonged cyclic life, rapid charging rate, high safety and simple structure [4–7]. Typically, flexible SCs are comprised of deformable electrodes and gel/solid polymer electrolyte, in which polymer electrolyte plays a crucial role in guarantying the safety of the device and integrating the whole device to maintain its mechanical and electrochemical stability regardless of the imposed deformation, effectively avoid the risks of leakage and combustion brought by conventional liquid electrolytes (LEs). In addition, compared to solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs) which possess low ionic conductivities (10–8–10–5 S cm−1) and poor adhesion to electrodes, gel polymer electrolytes (GPEs) endow SCs with deformable ability, good ionic conductivity (10–4–10–2 S cm−1) and great interfacial contact, enabling them be promising for flexible electronics [8–12].

To date, numerous polymers have been utilized in GPEs, such as poly(vinylalcohol) (PVA) [13], Poly(acrylonitrile) (PAN) [14], poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) [15] and poly(vinylidenefluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene) (PVDF-HFP) [16,17]. Besides, new polymers are coming to the attention of researchers for replacing conventional polymers for GPEs [18–20]. While PVDF-HFP is still regarded as one of the most prevailing polymers for GPEs blessed with affordable cost for scale application, favorable thermal stability and electrochemical stability in organic/ionic electrolyte under high voltage, which can realize the high energy density of SCs [21,22].

However, pure PVDF-HFP possesses comparatively low ionic conductivity and poor mechanical property, severely hindering the rate capability, cyclic stability and other electrochemical performance of SCs under deformation. Several attempts are carried out to enhance the performance of PVDF-HFP based GPEs [20–35]. Specifically, introducing additives into GPEs is the most common method. Graphene oxide (GO) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are effective additives that benefited from high surface area and abundant oxygen-containing functional group which could bring great electrochemical activity, promote the thermal, mechanical stability and ionic conductivity of GPEs and also decrease the interfacial resistance [23–25]. Beyond that, metal oxide fillers like Al2O3, TiO2, SiO2 etc. are also prevalent in polymer electrolyte. And the improvements of the GPEs have been shown in reducing crystallinity, strengthen mechanical performance and thermal stability, and promote ion transport [26–31]. Besides introducing fillers into electrolytes, constructing a dual-polymer matrix is a new trend to optimize the electrochemical performance of gel polymer electrolytes [32–38]. However, a GPE qualified with high ionic conductivity, mechanical and electrochemical stability at the same time is difficult to realize, which restrains its application in many areas.

Herein, to overcome the obstacles in the scalable applications of PVDF-HFP based GPEs which still face several imperfections, possible strategies of optimizing the properties of GPEs are combined to develop a comprehensively excellent gel polymer electrolyte by employing dual polymers as a matrix, introducing additives and applying UV cross-linking method in this work. In specific, PVDF-HFP is cross-linked with PAN by the medium of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) to build a stable structure of the GPE. Besides, N-CQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles are brought in as additives to improve the ionic transport, mechanical, thermal and electrochemical performance of the GPEs. The electrochemical performance of this GPE was evaluated by assembling coin cell and flexible SCs. This novel GPE based SCs possess large specific capacitance (102 F g−1) and great cyclic (85% capacitance retention at 1 A g−1 under 3.5 V after 20,000 cycles) and rate (105% capacitance retention at the current density of 40 A g−1) performance. Additionally, a superior energy density 42.88 Wh kg−1 with a power density of 19.3 kW kg−1 at the current density of 40 A g−1 has been achieved. Moreover, the GPE based flexible supercapacitor exhibits outstanding cycling stability at a bending state after 3000 cycles at the current density of 0.5 mA cm−2. This comprehensively well behaved GPE demonstrates the great prospect of expanding the application scenarios of flexible SCs.

1 g corn starch powder was added to 50 mL deionized water, 5 wt% urea was then dispersed in the starch solution followed by 30 min sonication. Then the suspension was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave to react under 180 ℃ for 10 h. After the reaction, the product was naturally cooled to room temperature, and transferred to the beaker to sonicate for 1 hour. Then the solution was vacuum-filtrated through a 0.22 mm microporous membrane to remove insoluble carbon product. The dialysis of the product was finished in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500) for 2 days to remove unfused small molecules. Finally, the product was obtained by freeze drying for 2 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of cross-linked PVDF-HFP composite gel polymer electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para: N-CQDs (1 wt% of the combined polymers) and TiO2 (10 wt% of the combined polymers) nanoparticles were first added in an amount of dimethylformamide (DMF) solution to stir for 2 h, then the combined polymers consisting of 12.5 wt% PAN and 87.5 wt% PVDF-HFP were dissolved in the mixed solution, respectively. After stirring for 12 h, the CIBA initiator was added to the solution and covered by tinfoil to stir 30 min. Later, the viscous solution was blade cast with the thickness of 200 μm on the PTFE membrane and followed by UV irradiation under UVLED machine (UVB 365 nm, XM210, Aventk) for 5 min with 50% light power. Then the film was transferred to a vacuum oven at 60 ℃ for 6 h to evaporate the residual solvent. The UV irradiated membrane was named P-N-12.5P-T (UV), and the unirradiated one was named P-N-12.5P-T (W/O UV). Finally, the film was cut with the diameter of 14 mm for CR2025 coin cell and 25 mm × 25 mm for flexible device and then immersed into 1 M MeEt3NBF4/Acetonitrile (AN) organic electrolyte for 2 h and wiped the surface of the GPE for 2.5 V CR2025 coin cell test, and (1 M MeEt3NBF4 + 1.5 M [PYR14][BF4])/AN electrolyte for 3.5 V coin cell test and flexible device test. For comparison, P-N-12.5P (UV) membrane was prepared without adding TiO2 nanoparticles.

A uniform slurry of the 80 wt% activated carbon (YP-50F), 10 wt% conducting carbon black (Super P) and 10 wt% binder (PVDF) was prepared by mixing them in NMP, followed by vigorous stirring for about 12 h. Then the slurry was blade cast onto the carbon-coated Al foil followed by vacuum dried at 80 ℃ for 12 h and punched into round disks with 12 mm diameter for CR2025 coin cell and 20 mm × 20 mm for the flexible device. The MAC500LC cellulose separator was used for comparison in coin cells. For the fabrication of a flexible supercapacitor, the gel polymer electrolyte was stacked by two electrodes and sealed with plastic films.

The cross-sectional morphologies of the membranes were conducted by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Phenom Pro X), and the Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was carried out by this machine and (SU6600, Hitachi) microscope with the energy of 15 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS, AXIS ULtrabld) was utilized to characterize the surface chemical analysis of the membranes. The thermal stability of the membranes with/without cross-linking were performed by thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) (TGA/DSC 1, METTLER TOLEDO) at a heating rate of 10 ℃ min−1 from 25 to 600 ℃ under N2 atmosphere. The mechanical property of the electrolyte membranes was measured using dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA, Q800, TA instruments) with the dimensions of the electrolyte sample are 0.5 cm × 2 cm × 20 µm. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the membranes were analyzed by FTIR spectrophotometer (IN10 + IZ10, Nicolet) in transmission/reflection mode. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) (DISCOVER DSC250, TA instrument) was performed with a heating rate of 10 ℃ min−1 from 100 ℃ to 400 ℃. The thermal stability test was performed in the muffle furnace (SL-1100, MTI Corporation) under 200 ℃ for 10 min. The dielectric properties of the composite membranes were measured using broadband dielectric spectroscopy (Concept80, NOVOCONTROL). The wettability test was conducted with a contact angle goniometer (DSA100, Germany). The cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) experiments performed with Stainless Steel (SS)/GPE/Ag cell (10 mV s−1) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out using BioLogic VMP3 electrochemical analyzer under ambient conditions. The impedance spectra were recorded by applying a sine wave with 10 mV amplitude over a frequency range of 100 kHz to 100 mHz for coin cell, 1 MHz to 100 mHz for the flexible device. The rate and cycling performance of SCs were conducted on an Arbin MSTAT4 electrochemical station.

The liquid electrolyte uptake is calculated using the following equation:
Uptake
( % )

W -
W

W
0
× 100 %

W 0
is the weight of the dried membranes, and
W
is the weight of the membranes infiltrated with electrolyte.

The energy density and power density of the device are calculated as the equations below:
(1)
E =
1 2
C cell
V 2
1 3600
(2)

E
t
D

In which
C cell
is the capacitance of the cell (F),
V
is the operational voltage (V),
E
is the energy density of the cell (Wh kg−1),
t D
is the discharging time of the cell (s),
P
is the power density of the cell (kW kg−1).

Fig. 1 describes the synthetic routes of the gel polymer electrolyte. The doctor blade casting method was applied to prepare the uniform and thickness-adjustable polymer membrane. The combination of two polymers and the introduction of additives (N-CQDs and TiO2) in this work are intended to optimize the ionic conductivity and electrochemical stability of the GPEs. In the process of UV irradiation, the CIBA works as photoinitiator (IRGACURE 819), and the N-CQDs enriched with oxygen-containing functional groups as demonstrated in TEM images (Fig S1), cross-linking with PVDF-HFP and PAN under the action of its photocatalytic characteristic to lower the degree of crystallinity of polymers and building a larger network so as to increase the movement of polymer segments and facilitate the ionic transport [39]. And the nano TiO2 particles are regarded as a photocatalyst to accelerate the cross-linking process as well. After immersing into the liquid electrolyte (LE), the cross-linked electrolyte membrane turns into a gel polymer electrolyte containing a lot of liquid electrolyte.

The mechanical, thermal and electrochemical performance of the membrane is strongly correlated to the structure and morphology of the polymer matrix, which can be adjusted by UV irradiation. The cross-sectional morphologies of the membranes with and without UV irradiation are observed in Fig. 2 . The nomenclature of the membrane relies on its composition, and the duration of UV irradiation is referred to by a previously reported work [40]. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the membrane without irradiation is much denser with no pore. For comparison, the membrane exposed to the UV light becomes considerably loose and porous (Fig. 2b), which is desirable for GPE to absorb more LE than the liquid uptake shown in Fig. S2. The large round PAN particles are covered by TiO2, and N-CQDs cross-linked with PVDF-HFP and PAN to work as the matrix of the membrane, as the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental maps of the UV irradiated membrane shown in Fig. S3. To explain the creation of this special morphology, we compared the cross-sectional morphologies of membranes with different compositions irradiated by UV light; it is obvious that the TiO2 nanoparticles disperse evenly in the membrane which contains no PAN (Fig. S4b and d). While as long as the PAN participates in the membrane, TiO2 nanoparticles are inclined to surround PAN particles (Fig. S4c). And the uniformly dispersed N-CQDs with PVDF-HFP under UV irradiation can create a network structure as shown in Fig. S4d. On this basis, the introduction of PAN in the membrane promotes the formation of porous structure (Fig. 2b), which we believe it is the effect of N-CQDs which act as a medium in the process of cross-linking to connect with PVDF-HFP and PAN.

To substantiate the cross-linking mechanism of the membrane exposed to the UV light, the detailed chemical structure characterization of the membranes is disclosed by XPS as shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. S5. According to the comparison in Fig. S5a, the intensities of C and O peaks of the membranes vary notably before and after UV irradiation. According to Fig. 3(a and d), the C-C peak (284.8 eV) is drastically intensified, C-O peak increases slightly, while CH2 (286.4 eV), C–F/CO (289.4 eV) and CF2 (290.9 eV) peaks all decline after the UV irradiation [41]. The peak intensity variation of different bonds points to the cross-linking reaction, in which the CF2 bond of PVDF-HFP breaks down, and the carbon atoms connected to the functional groups of N-CQDs which will be further proved by FTIR spectra. In addition, the increase of the C-C and the decline of CO peak intensities substantiate the breakdown of the CO bonds in N-CQDs and the connection with PAN, Fig. 3(b and e) also confirm this statement. In the deconvoluted N 1 s peaks of two membranes (Fig. 3c and f), besides from the CN peak (398 eV) attributed to PAN, the detected peak of the protonation of amino groups (401 eV) in UV irradiated membrane proves the cross-linking of N-CQDs with PAN [42]. Moreover, Fig. S5 demonstrates the peak intensity variation of different bonds on the surface of the membranes with different materials addition before UV irradiation. As shown in Fig. S5b-d, the C-C peak first increases after mixing PAN with PVDF-HFP, and then drops at the time TiO2 is added to the membrane. CF2/CF3/C–F peaks all decline at the beginning and are followed by a rapid rise when introducing PAN and TiO2 in order; this verifies that in the absence of TiO2, PAN and PVDF-HFP distribute evenly in the membrane; otherwise TiO2 nanoparticles tend to cover on the surface of the PAN, leading to the decrease of C-C peak.

Apart from the XPS characterization, the FTIR spectra in Fig. 4 (a) further describe the cross-linking process of the membrane, the peaks at 614, 762, 796, 840, 976, 1174 and 1381 cm−1 are attributed to CF2 vibration, CH2 rocking vibrations, CF3 stretching vibration, amorphous phase belt of PVDF-HFP, C–F stretching vibrations, –C-C-C– symmetric stretching and CH2 wagging respectively. After exposure to the UV light, the peaks of CF2 vibration (614 cm−1), CH2 rocking vibrations (762 cm−1), CF3 stretching vibration (796 cm−1), C–F stretching vibrations (976 cm−1) and CH2 wagging (1381 cm−1) all become weakened, while the peak of an amorphous belt of PVDF-HFP (840 cm−1) gets stronger, and the peak of –C-C-C– bond symmetric stretching (1174 cm−1) behaves an obvious red shift, which all substantiate the cross-linking of N-CQDs and PVDF-HFP under UV irradiation [43,44]. Besides, the disappeared peaks of CO in the amine group (1661 cm−1) and NH2 (1540 cm−1) attributed to N-CQDs (Fig. S6) and the arisen CN peak (1590 cm−1) (Fig. S7) indicate the chemical reactions of N-CQDs with PAN and PVDF-HFP as well [45–48], and the chemical reaction can be described as shown in Fig. S8. Beyond that, the cross-linking reaction can also be proved by studying the thermal behavior of membranes before and after UV exposure in DSC curves (Fig. 4b). The exothermic peak of P-N-12.5P-T (UV) membrane centered at 341 ℃ is assigned as the cyclization reaction of PAN. While the P-N-12.5P-T (W/O UV) sample doesn’t exhibit an exothermic peak at this temperature, it is attributed to the coverage of stable TiO2 nanoparticles which impedes the cyclization reaction of PAN. However, the cross-linking process endows PAN with oxygen-containing functional groups originating from N-CQDs, promoting the ionic cyclization reaction at a lower temperature; this explains the existence of the exothermic peak of P-N-12.5P-T (UV) membrane at this temperature range. Fig. S9 compares the DSC curves of P-N-12.5P-T (UV) and P-N-12.5P (UV) samples, certifying the cross-linking can facilitate the cyclization reaction of PAN, and TiO2 coverage is the reason for mitigating the cyclization reaction [49,50]. Fig. S10 displays the shift of melting peak of membranes with different constituents, indicating that TiO2 nanoparticles and N-CQDs can decrease the degree of crystallinity of the membrane. The high thermal stability is quite significant to guarantee the safety and reliability of membranes applied in SCs, and the dimensional thermal stability of membranes is evaluated by comparing the dimensional shrinkage of the membranes after 10 minutes of baking in the air under 200 ℃, it is apparent that the UV irradiated membrane exhibits better thermal stability as depicted in Fig. S11. Furthermore, the TGA curves can certify the thermal-stable superiority of UV irradiated membrane as displayed in Fig. 4(c), it can be seen that the UV irradiated membrane exhibits a higher decomposition temperature between 380 ℃ and 410 ℃, and more residual weight after 400 ℃, ensuring a safer device operational environment provided by the as-prepared GPE. Along with the thermal stability, the mechanical property of the membranes is also a pivotal parameter to promise the safety of the devices, which is performed by tensile stress–strain test shown in Fig. 4(d), the cross-linked membrane possesses higher elongation but lower tensile strength compared to PVDF-HFP membrane and the prepared membrane without UV irradiation, showing a great prospect in the application of foldable and stretchable devices. The frequency dependence of the relative permittivity of the membranes before and after cross-linking is shown in Fig. 4(e). The relative permittivity can reflect the dielectric properties of the sample and indirectly indicate the change of the sample structure. The extension of the molecular chain during the cross-linking process reduces the regularity of the polymer chain, making the arrangements of the polymer chain segment more irregular, leading to the increase of intermolecular space and the drop of dielectric constant at low frequency. At the same time, after cross-linking, a large number of uniformly dispersed pores are formed inside the membrane, which increases the air volume rate and reduces its dielectric constant [51–53]. While the mild decline of dielectric constant has little to do with the compatibility of electrolyte and membrane.

The cross-linked GPE based coin type SC is tested to confirm its electrochemical feasibility. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy is performed to analyze the electrolyte/electrode interface properties of the SCs. The Nyquist plots for three samples in the frequency range of 0.1–100 kHz are exhibited in Fig. 5 (a); The SC fabricated with UV irradiated sample exhibits a lower charge transfer resistance (R ct:7.848 Ω) and higher ionic conductivity compared to GPEs without UV irradiation or the addition of N-CQDs (Fig. S12), owning to the more amorphous structure of cross-linked polymers which facilitate the ionic transport. The controlled experiments have been made to determine the content of PAN in the membrane. From the Nyquist plots in Fig. S13, it shows that when PAN is taken 12.5 wt% of the two polymers, it provides the lowest R ct (8.81 Ω) for GPEs. Moreover, the wetting property comparison of the membranes with and without UV irradiation can indirectly certified the UV irradiated membrane possesses higher ionic conductivity. The smaller contact angle after 10 seconds demonstrates the preferable wettability and interfacial contact of P-N-12.5P-T (UV) sample (Fig. S14). The cyclic voltammograms (CV) curves of two samples substantiate the superiority of UV irradiated sample in ionic transport, showing a much more rectangular shape even at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 compared to the unirradiated one (Fig. 5b and c). The excellent rate performance of UV irradiated sample at a higher current density (10 A/g) is attributed to the generation of effective ion-diffusion paths by the porous structure of GPEs [54,55]. Besides, the mitigated concentration polarization plays a pivotal role in the fast discharge reaction, the dielectric material TiO2 promotes the dissociation of the electrolyte salt, increasing the ion concentration of the electrolyte and reducing the ion concentration gradient, thereby weakening the ionic concentration polarization and providing enough ions for the electrode to absorb under high current density (Fig. 5d) [56–58]. While the inferior rate performance of the unirradiated sample and commercial cellulose separator is due to the sluggish transport of ions at high current density triggered by high concentration polarization. Thus the cycling performance of UV irradiated one at high current density is prominently superior to the other two samples (Fig. 5e).

To examine the stability of the GPEs under high operational voltage, comparisons of four samples are made under 3.5 V by applying self-prepared electrolyte which combines the amount of commercial electrolyte with 1.5 M ionic liquid. The Nyquist plots of four samples in Fig. 6 (a) demonstrate the outstanding ionic transport of P-N-12.5P-T (UV) sample, certifying the significance of TiO2 in enhancing the ionic conductivity. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurement performed in Fig. 6(b) shows the much wider electrochemical window of P-N-12.5P-T (UV) sample, this is mainly attributed to 1) the structural stability of cross-linked membrane, which can restrain the side reaction of the N-CQDs under high voltage [59]; 2) the dielectric materials like TiO2 are able to improve the electrochemical stability of the GPEs [60–62]. And this is also notarized by CV curves of three GPE samples at the scan rate of 5 mV s−1 under 3.5 V in Fig. S15. With the assistance of TiO2 nanoparticles and UV cross-linking method, the P-N-12.5P-T (UV) sample manifests prominent rate performance (105% capacitance retention at the current density of 40 A g−1) (Fig. 6d) and cyclic stability (85% capacitance retention after 20,000 cycles) under the operational voltage of 3.5 V (Fig. 6c). Besides, the symmetrical GCD plots of P-N-12.5P-T (UV) sample under various operational voltages also indicate its electrochemical stability (Fig. S16). Notably, the P-N-12.5P-T (UV) sample based SC harvests a remarkable energy density of 42.88 Wh kg−1 even at the power supply of 19.3 kW kg−1, which is highly superior to most reported carbons-based quasi-solid-state SCs (Fig. 6e) [23,63–68].

The remarkable performance of small-sized flexible SC devices under deformation is the premise to realize the extensive application of the GPE. Therefore, the mechanical flexibility of the device was investigated by measuring the electrochemical performance of the device under a bending state with a radius of 12.5 mm. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the CV curves of P-N-12.5P-T (UV) sample at a high scan rate (500 mV s−1) are well persisted with rectangular shape under flat and bending state. While the unirradiated sample demonstrates a more elliptical shape of CV curves under bending or even flat state, which is consistent with the result of the Nyquist plots of two samples in Fig. S17 and Fig. S18. The capacitance retention plays a critical role in evaluating the rate performance of SCs. As expected, the P-N-12.5P-T (UV) sample displays preferable stability in rate performance regardless of testing under a flat or deformed state. To compare, the unirradiated one shows a severe decay in capacitance at high current density, elucidating the significance of UV cross-linking for polymers used in GPEs (Fig. 7b). And the P-N-12.5P-T (UV) based SC can keep illumining blue light-emitting diode (LED) lights whenever the device is flat or deformed as depicted in Fig. 7(c). The cycling performance of two samples under bending state is demonstrated in Fig. 7(d), the unirradiated sample shows an inferior cyclic performance, which is unable to discharge after 1000 cycles. While the irradiated counterpart based flexible SC presents a slow capacitance fading and can retain more than 90% of the initial capacitance after 3000 cycles, and the LED lights stay on for 120 s after charging the SC to 3.5 V. The excellent electrochemical and mechanical performance of the P-N-12.5P-T (UV) GPE suggests its prominence in the application of flexible devices.

In summary, a novel composite polymer membrane implemented for GPEs has been successfully fabricated by cross-linking N-CQDs with PVDF-HFP and PAN to build a polymer matrix with the help of UV irradiation, as well as introducing additives (N-CQDs and TiO2) to the membrane; which could effectively improve the ionic conductivity and electrochemical stability under high voltage. Apart from the excellent thermal and mechanical stability of the P-N-12.5P-T (UV) membrane, the remarkable rate (105% capacitance retention at the current density of 40 A g−1 under 3.5 V) and cyclic performance (85% at 1 A g−1 under 3.5 V after 20,000 cycles) of P-N-12.5P-T (UV) based supercapacitor (SC) also elucidate the superiority of the UV irradiated membrane prepared in this work. Besides, the high energy density (42.88 Wh kg−1) of P-N-12.5P-T (UV) based SC is well retained even at the high power supply (19.3 kW kg−1). More importantly, the well behaved P-N-12.5P-T (UV) based flexible SC under rate and cycling tests makes it more competitive in the advanced flexible devices, providing more application prospects for GPEs in an energy storage system.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the funding supports from the Natural Science Basis Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (2019JLZ-10), and the Independent Research Project of National Key Laboratory of Electrical Insulation and Power Equipment (EIPE19111). The authors are grateful to Instrumental Analysis Center of Xi'an Jiaotong University for technical assistance. SEM was carried out at the Center for Advancing Materials Performance from the Nanoscale (CAMP NANO). We thank Dr. Chaowei Guo for his help with SEM.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jechem.2022.09.015.
